name: Fly.io MCP Server
what_it_does: Official Fly.io MCP server enabling AI agents to interact with Fly.io's distributed application platform — deploying and scaling Fly apps, managing Machines, querying app status, and operating globally distributed services.
use_cases: Querying Fly app deployment status from CI/CD agents
Scaling Fly Machines up or down from capacity management agents
Managing secrets and environment configuration for Fly apps via agents
Checking Machine health and logs for incident response agents
Creating and managing Fly volumes and Postgres clusters
not_for: Teams using AWS, GCP, Azure, Render, or other deployment platforms
Traditional server management requiring SSH-first workflows
best_when: An agent needs to deploy, scale, or manage applications on Fly.io — particularly for globally distributed or edge-deployed applications.
avoid_when: You're using a different deployment platform — use their respective MCP or API.

auth.notes: Fly access token via FLY_API_TOKEN. Deploy tokens can be scoped to specific apps. Personal tokens for full account access.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Free allowance: 3 shared-cpu-1x VMs, 3GB persistent storage
pricing.paid_tiers: Pay-as-you-go by Machine compute and storage
pricing.notes: Credit card required to access full free allowance. Pay-as-you-go pricing is developer-friendly.

performance.rate_limits: API rate limits apply but not prominently documented

known_agent_gotchas: Fly uses Machines API (lower level) vs flyctl commands — agents need to understand both
App-scoped deploy tokens available — use these instead of personal tokens for CI/CD agents
Fly Machines can be stopped/started independently — understand VM lifecycle
Fly Postgres is a separate product from app deployments
flyctl CLI used for many operations — direct API support varies
agent_readiness.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. App-scoped deploy tokens provide better isolation than personal tokens. SOC 2 Type II. Good security for a developer PaaS.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. App-scoped tokens are a good pattern. SOC 2 Type II. Adequate security for developer PaaS.
